skill.name: managing-task-lifecycle
skill.description: Guide agents through PairCoder task status changes, Trello task commands, completion checks, and lifecycle validation steps.
skill.summary: Manages PairCoder task starts, progress updates, blocks, completion, and Trello-linked workflow transitions.
skill.author: BPSAI

security_audit.summary: Static analysis produced many command, network, filesystem, and credential indicators, but most are Markdown command examples or environment variable documentation. The confirmed risk is legitimate workflow automation: the helper script runs fixed local commands, and the skill instructs agents to use PairCoder, Trello, GitHub, git, test, and lint commands. No prompt injection, obfuscation, credential exfiltration, or malicious intent was found.

medium_findings.title: Helper Script Runs Local Project Commands
medium_findings.description: TRUE POSITIVE. The completion helper invokes subprocess.run for pytest, ruff, and git status. The commands are fixed, but tests and lint checks can execute project-controlled code.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: Direct subprocess usage is present with hardcoded command arrays. Confidence is high for command execution, but lower for abuse because no shell string or user-controlled command is used.

medium_findings.title: Lifecycle Commands Can Modify Task And Trello State
medium_findings.description: TRUE POSITIVE. The skill instructs agents to run PairCoder task and ttask commands that can update local task files, trigger hooks, and move Trello cards.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The documented behavior clearly changes workflow state and may call remote Trello services. It appears intended and transparent, not covert or malicious.

medium_findings.title: Full CLI Reference Includes Broad Operational Commands
medium_findings.description: TRUE POSITIVE. The reference lists install, migration, webhook, git, GitHub, Trello, and MCP commands. These can affect files, repositories, or remote services if executed.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are documentation examples rather than automatic execution. They still expand the operational scope agents may follow when using this skill.

low_findings.title: Markdown Backticks Misclassified As Ruby Shell Execution
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE. Most external command alerts are inline Markdown code spans or fenced shell examples documenting PairCoder commands, not Ruby backtick execution.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited files are Markdown and the surrounding context is command documentation. No Ruby code or shell interpolation was found at these locations.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URL Alerts Are Documentation Examples
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE. The URL findings are placeholder repository, pull request, or Trello links in documentation. No code sends data to these URLs.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The locations show example values used in reference tables or command snippets. I did not find code performing network requests to those URLs.

low_findings.title: Path Traversal And Weak Crypto Labels Are Documentation Artifacts
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE. The path traversal alert points to a Markdown relative link, and weak cryptography alerts point to words or extensions in documentation.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited contexts do not contain hashing or cryptographic operations. The relative path is a documentation link, not a file access operation.

low_findings.title: Environment Variable Names Are Credential Documentation
low_findings.description: NEEDS REVIEW. The reference lists Trello, GitHub, and Toggl token variable names, but no code in this skill reads or exports them.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The credential names are real operational inputs for the PairCoder CLI, but this skill only documents them. Human review should confirm marketplace warnings cover token handling.

dangerous_patterns.title: Fixed subprocess.run Command Execution
dangerous_patterns.description: The helper script runs local project commands through subprocess.run. This is bounded by hardcoded argument lists but still executes repository tests and tooling.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The code directly calls subprocess.run. The risk is moderated because command names and arguments are fixed arrays, not shell strings.

dangerous_patterns.title: Agent-Guided External CLI Operations
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill tells agents to run PairCoder lifecycle commands instead of only editing text. These commands can trigger hooks and service synchronization.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The behavior is explicit in the skill instructions. The commands are normal for the workflow, but they can change local and remote state.

dangerous_patterns.title: Git And Remote Service Workflow Commands
dangerous_patterns.description: The CLI reference includes git add, commit, push, Trello, GitHub, and webhook commands. These commands should require user confirmation in sensitive repositories.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands are documented examples, not hidden code. They are still potentially impactful if an agent follows them automatically.

content.value_statement: Task status changes can miss hooks, timers, Trello sync, or completion checks. This skill gives agents a consistent workflow for starting, updating, blocking, validating, and completing PairCoder tasks.
actual_capabilities: Chooses between local task commands and Trello task commands based on task identifiers.
Provides commands for starting, commenting, completing, and blocking tasks.
Explains PairCoder hooks for timers, metrics, Trello sync, and state updates.
Includes helper scripts to validate task files and completion readiness.
Documents Trello card conventions, acceptance criteria workflow, and PR URL handling.
Links to a broad PairCoder CLI reference for related workflow commands.
limitations: Requires the PairCoder CLI and project structure to be installed separately.
Does not verify that Trello, GitHub, or Toggl credentials are configured correctly.
Can affect task files, git state, or Trello cards when commands are executed.
The conventions are specific to BPSAI PairCoder workflows and may need adaptation.

use_cases.title: Start Assigned Work Correctly
use_cases.description: Use the right PairCoder command to mark a task in progress and trigger lifecycle hooks.

use_cases.title: Complete Trello-Linked Tasks
use_cases.description: Move Trello cards to done, check acceptance criteria, and add completion summaries through the CLI.

use_cases.title: Validate Task Readiness
use_cases.description: Run task file, test, lint, and git status checks before marking work complete.

prompt_templates.title: Start A Local Task
prompt_templates.scenario: Begin work on a PairCoder task file.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the task lifecycle skill to start TASK-123. Tell me which command you will run and what hooks should fire.

prompt_templates.title: Update Trello Progress
prompt_templates.scenario: Add a progress note without changing status.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the task lifecycle skill to add a Trello progress comment for TRELLO-abc123: API endpoints are complete and tests are next.

prompt_templates.title: Complete With Checks
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the task lifecycle skill to validate TASK-123, summarize completion steps, and choose the correct done command for a Trello project.

prompt_templates.title: Recover A Blocked Workflow
prompt_templates.scenario: Mark work blocked with clear context.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the task lifecycle skill to block TRELLO-abc123 because the staging API is unavailable. Include the command and the expected state changes.

output_examples.input: Start TASK-042 in a project without Trello.
output_examples.output: Recommended action: run the local task update command with status in progress. Expected result: task state updates and lifecycle hooks run.

output_examples.input: Complete TRELLO-abc123 after implementation is done.
output_examples.output: Recommended action: use the Trello task done workflow with a completion summary. Expected result: acceptance criteria are checked and the card moves to done.

output_examples.input: Check whether TASK-042 is ready to close.
output_examples.output: Recommended action: run the completion readiness helper. Review task format, tests, linting, and git status before changing status.

best_practices: Confirm whether a task is local or Trello-linked before choosing commands.
Run validation checks before completing a task or moving a card to done.
Use progress comments for visibility without changing task status.
anti_patterns: Do not edit task status files directly when lifecycle hooks must run.
Do not mark Trello cards done before acceptance criteria are checked.
Do not run git, Trello, or credential-dependent commands without user approval.

faq.question: What does this skill manage?
faq.answer: It manages PairCoder task lifecycle actions, including start, progress, block, validation, and completion workflows.

faq.question: Does it require Trello?
faq.answer: No. It supports local task commands and Trello task commands, depending on the project setup.

faq.question: Can it run commands automatically?
faq.answer: It provides command guidance and includes validation scripts. Users should approve commands that change files or remote services.

faq.question: What external services are involved?
faq.answer: The documented workflow can involve Trello, GitHub, and Toggl through the PairCoder CLI and configured credentials.

faq.question: Why is the audit risk medium?
faq.answer: The skill has legitimate automation, but it can execute local checks and guide changes to task, git, and Trello state.

faq.question: Is malicious behavior confirmed?
faq.answer: No. The review found no prompt injection, obfuscation, credential exfiltration, or hidden malicious intent.
